content.subtitle: Relative pronouns
items: which (for things and animals): Did you see the film which was on TV last night?
who (for people): Tom Davies, who is appearing in concert in Reading this week, is with me in the studio.
when (for times): Do you remember the day when we met?
where (for places): This is the place where they filmed Citizen Kane.
why (for reasons): That's the reason why he's so popular.
whom (for people as the object of the relative clause): Is that the man whom we saw at the cinema yesterday?
whose (for possession): My next guest on the show is John Travolta, whose career goes back to the early seventies.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: When the relative pronoun is the subject of the relative clause, you do not need another subject: ✓ I admire Jude Law, who always works hard on his films.

Whom is quite formal. Informally, we use who instead. After a preposition, we always use whom: Charlie Chaplin was a comic genius to whom all comedians owe a great deal. (formal) / Charlie Chaplin was a comic genius who all comedians owe a great deal to. (informal)
Where can be replaced by a preposition + which: The theatre where / in which I first acted is somewhere around here.
When can be replaced by a preposition + which: Do you know the year when / in which the first western was made?

content.subtitle: Use
items: To give extra information about something/someone: Ray Watson, who starred in Bandits, is considering making a film based on the life of Einstein.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: Non-defining relative clauses are separated from the rest of the sentence by commas.
We cannot leave out the word which introduces the relative clause and we cannot use the word 'that' instead.

Which can refer back to the whole of the sentence: We finally got tickets for the concert, which was very lucky. (It doesn't mean the concert was lucky. It means getting tickets for the concert was lucky.)

content.subtitle: Use
items: To tell us which one of a group of things/people we are talking about: The book which I've read was the best of all. / The one who is wearing a blue shirt is Justin Timberlake, isn't it?

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: Defining relative clauses are not separated from the rest of the sentence by commas.
We can also use 'that' to introduce the relative clause: This is the DVD that I told you about the other day.
We can often leave out the word which introduces the relative clause when it is the object of the clause: This is the DVD I told you about the other day.
We do not need a preposition when we use 'where' or 'when': The theatre where I first acted is somewhere around here.

content.subtitle: Forms
items: Present participles end in -ing.
Past participles usually end in -ed, although there are many irregular verbs.
Perfect participles are formed using having + past participle.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To follow prepositions and conjunctions (present and perfect participles): By appearing in that cigarette advert, he damaged his acting career. / After playing / having played video games all morning, I was really tired.

To explain the reason for something (present and perfect participles): Being quite good looking, Ralph decided to make a career as a model. / Having seen the film before, I knew what was going to happen.
To talk about actions happening at the same time (present participles): Waiting for the show to begin, I felt really nervous.

To replace some relative clauses (present and past participles): Imagine being the person directing a big budget film! (= Imagine being the person who is directing a big budget film!) / The person chosen for the part will be contacted by phone. (= The person who is chosen for the part will be contacted by phone.)
To talk about past actions happening in sequence (perfect participles): Having finished my homework, I decided to go to the cinema.
As an alternative passive form (past participles): Made to wait, the actor began to get very annoyed.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: The participle and the rest of the sentence must both refer to the same subject.
examples: ✓ Watching TV, I saw a news report about Hollywood.
✗ Watching TV, a news report came on about Hollywood.

sections.title: A: Relative pronouns
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: The minister, **which / who** was appointed just last week, made no comment on the situation.
Isn't that the spot **which / where** the accident happened last night?
The human brain, **which / who** weighs about 1400 grammes, is ten times the size of a baboon's.
There are several reasons **which / why** I don't want to see Michael tonight.
This is the office **which / where** I work.
The new girl in our class, **who's / whose** name is Alexandra, seems really nice.
The Titanic, **which / that** people said was unsinkable, sank on her maiden voyage.
All the people to **who / whom** the e-mail was sent replied.
April 1st, **which / when** we play tricks on people, is known as April Fools' Day.
Harry Hill, **who / whose** new series starts next week, is one of my favourite comedians.
A very popular breed of dog is the German Shepherd, **which / who** is often used as a guard dog.
Blackpool Tower, **which / that** was modelled on the Eiffel Tower, is a very well-known landmark.

sections.title: B: Error correction (Relative clauses)
sections.instructions: If a word in bold is correct, put a tick (✓). If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: November 5th was the day **which** Guy Fawkes tried to blow up Parliament.
There were none of my favourite biscuits left when I had a cup of tea, **that** was really annoying.
I've just got a new webcam **that** takes really clear pictures.
The Godfather was made by Francis Ford Copolla, **who's** daughter is also a film director.
Can you think of any reason **which** Cathy should be angry with me?
Here's a photo of the hotel where we **stayed in** when we were in Beijing.
How do you think the first man **whom** walked on the moon felt?
This is the first occasion on **which** the leaders of these two countries have met.
He is a person for **who** very few people feel much sympathy.
The moment **which** the hero suddenly appears from behind a tree is the best moment in the whole film.
I met a man **which** had a dog with only three legs.
My new school, **who** I moved to about a month ago, seems like quite a friendly place.

sections.title: C: Rewrite as one sentence using a relative clause
sections.instructions: Rewrite as one sentence using a relative clause.

items.sentence: Prince Charles is heir to the throne of England. His wife was Princess Diana.
items.answer: Prince Charles, whose wife was Princess Diana, is heir to the throne of England.

items.sentence: Microsoft has a lot of power in the world of computers. That annoys some people.
items.answer: Microsoft has a lot of power in the world of computers, which annoys some people.

items.sentence: The euro replaced a number of national currencies. It was introduced in January 2002.
items.answer: The euro, which was introduced in January 2002, replaced a number of national currencies.

items.sentence: Friends is one of my favourite series. It ran for ten years.
items.answer: Friends, which is one of my favourite series, ran for ten years.

items.sentence: Venus is a very hot place. It is much closer to the Sun than the Earth is.
items.answer: Venus, which is much closer to the Sun than the Earth is, is a very hot place.

items.sentence: Cricket is popular in many countries of the world. It is played between two teams of eleven.
items.answer: Cricket, which is played between two teams of eleven, is popular in many countries of the world.

items.sentence: Parts of Buckingham Palace are open to the public. The queen lives there.
items.answer: Parts of Buckingham Palace, where the queen lives, are open to the public.

items.sentence: The greyhound is the fastest dog. It can reach speeds of over 65 kilometres an hour.
items.answer: The greyhound, which can reach speeds of over 65 kilometres an hour, is the fastest dog.

items.sentence: 1984 was written by George Orwell. His real name was Eric Blair.
items.answer: 1984 was written by George Orwell, whose real name was Eric Blair.

sections.title: D: Relative pronouns (gap fill)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap. Do not write that and do not leave any gaps blank.

items.sentence: I'm sure there was a reason (1) ...... I became a TV reviewer.
Maybe it was my love of classic shows (2) ...... kept me entertained as a child.
Or maybe it was the presenters (3) ...... seemed to promise an exciting world of glamour and showbiz.
I've watched so many shows lately (4) ...... have bored me to tears that I've forgotten.
Take Kid Swap, for example, the new show (5) ...... Channel 7 hopes will revive their viewing figures.
The show takes kids (6) ...... parents have had enough and swaps them with kids from other families for two weeks.
The problem with this show is that I'm just not sure at (7) ...... it's aimed - parents or teenagers.
Parents (8) ...... watch this show will find it childish, particularly at the moment (9) ...... one of the teenagers does nothing but complain.
Teenage viewers (10) ...... watch more than the first five minutes will soon get bored of the parents, (11) ...... blame anybody but themselves for the situation.
This is definitely one idea (12) ...... should be put exactly where it belongs - in the bin!
'Who's that over there?' 'Oh, it's our new teacher, ...... just started work today.'
'Which CD did you get Marcus in the end?' 'I got him the one ...... said he really wanted to hear.'
'Who did you send a Valentine's card to?' 'I'm not telling you, but it was someone ...... name begins with "B"?'
'Have you seen Jason Green's latest film?' 'Is that the one in ...... joins the FBI?'
'Why do you like Tania so much?' 'Well, she's one of the few people to ...... I can really talk.'
'Why don't we go to Lionel's for dinner tonight?' 'Is that the new restaurant ...... has just opened on the other side of town?'
'Could you lend me some money?' 'I'd like you to give me one good reason ...... I should.'
'What do you want to do this summer?' 'I think we should go somewhere ...... has plenty of sun and sand.'

sections.title: F: Omit the relative pronoun
sections.instructions: There are 9 relative pronouns in the text which are not necessary. Click on them to mark them.
sections.passage: The number of options [[that]] are available to us is increasing all the time. While previous generations, [[who]] lived before the electronic age, had musical instruments and card games [[that]] they played to pass the time, we have PlayStation, computers, televisions and stereos. Although parents [[whose]] teenagers spend a lot of time on these activities might worry about the effect [[which]] it is having on them, for most of us they provide harmless fun, [[which]] is becoming more important as life becomes more stressful. The television programmes [[which]] we watch and the computer games [[that]] we play all entertain us and, although there will always be people to [[whom]] they seem a complete waste of time, the effect [[that]] they have on society cannot be ignored. Entertainment is something [[which]] we all need and, as long as we're willing to pay, there will always be someone [[who]] is willing to provide it.

sections.title: G: Participles
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Before **meeting / met** Harriet, I never knew what real love was.
I'm sure that after **passing / passed** your exam, you'll feel a lot better.
Having **finishing / finished** the letter, Peter printed it out.
**Looking / Looked** through the window, I saw a strange man at the door.
I cooked dinner and, **doing / having done** that, I sat down to watch TV.
**Hearing / Heard** a noise outside, Janice went to investigate.
**Having lost / Losing** my notes, I couldn't revise properly for the test.
Having **been / Being** quite intelligent, Matt hopes to get into a good university.
**Having missed / Missing** the start of the film, I decided not to bother watching the rest of it.
After **making / made** her bed, Cherie had a shower.

sections.title: H: Participle transformations
sections.instructions: Rewrite the sentences using a participle clause.

items.sentence: I suddenly remembered where I'd left my keys when I was waiting for the bus.
items.answer: Waiting for the bus, I suddenly remembered where I'd left my keys.

items.sentence: I slipped because I was trying to run in the rain.
items.answer: Trying to run in the rain, I slipped.

items.sentence: I felt quite sorry for Tim when I saw him cry.
items.answer: Seeing Tim cry, I felt quite sorry for him.

items.sentence: John was depressed because he knew he was going to fail the exam.
items.answer: Knowing (that) he was going to fail the exam, John was depressed.

items.sentence: The reason I'm quite good at basketball is that I'm quite tall.
items.answer: Being quite tall, I'm quite good at basketball.

items.sentence: Georgia didn't know any Japanese so she used a phrase book when she was there.
items.answer: Not knowing any Japanese, Georgia used a phrase book when she was there.

items.sentence: Ed can afford three holidays abroad each year because he has a lot of money.
items.answer: Having a lot of money, Ed can afford three holidays abroad every year.

items.sentence: Maria began to worry when she realised she was lost.
items.answer: Realising (that) she was lost, Maria began to worry.

sections.title: I: Error correction (Participles)
sections.instructions: Tick (✓) the correct sentences. If a sentence is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Looking through the window, a **plane was passing** overhead.
items.answer: Looking through the window, I saw a plane passing overhead.

items.sentence: Realising that it was late, I ran all the way home.
Having examined me, **I was given a prescription** by the doctor.
items.answer: Having examined me, the doctor gave me a prescription.

items.sentence: Turning on the television, **the newsreader said** there had been an explosion.
items.answer: Turning on the television, I heard the newsreader say there had been an explosion.

items.sentence: Dropping the shopping bags on the kitchen floor, Mrs Moorcroft sighed and sat down heavily.
Having practised the piano, I decided to call one of my friends.

sections.title: J: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Each line either has one extra word that does not belong, or is correct. Write the extra word, or write OK if the line is correct.

items.sentence: Hello, viewers, and welcome to Easy Money, the game show who you
voted the best on TV! Now, remember yesterday when our current champion,
Pat Barber, having been won ten thousand euros, decided to carry on?
Well, she's here tonight and she's going for the star prize, which it is a brand new sports car!
That's right. Having got her this far, if Pat can answer three more questions correctly, then she could be driving home in this car, ladies and gentlemen.
Having not seen the programme before, you will know just how
difficult which it can be. I know Pat's a little nervous, but I'm sure she'll be
fine and there's one person for whom tonight is very special and that's Pat's
husband, Frank, who he is sitting in the audience. So, let's play Easy Money!
